event: Paleo-Indian hunter-gatherers settle the highlands and lowlands of present-day Guatemala, leaving behind stone tools such as Clovis points.
Early agricultural communities begin cultivating maize, beans, and squash, laying the foundation for sedentary Mesoamerican society.
Permanent farming villages and early pottery appear during the Preclassic period across the Maya region.
The Maya city of Kaminaljuyu rises in the central highlands as a major trade and ceremonial center.
El Mirador flourishes as one of the largest early Maya cities, building massive pyramids such as La Danta.
The Classic Maya period begins, marked by monumental architecture, hieroglyphic writing, and powerful rival city-states.
Tikal comes under the influence of Teotihuacan in a pivotal political intervention recorded on its monuments.
Tikal is defeated by the rival city of Caracol, beginning a period of decline before its later resurgence.
Tikal, under ruler Jasaw Chan Kawiil I, defeats Calakmul and enters a golden age of construction and power.
The Classic Maya civilization collapses across the southern lowlands as great cities like Tikal are gradually abandoned.
Postclassic highland Maya kingdoms such as the K'iche', Kaqchikel, and Tz'utujil consolidate power in fortified centers.
The K'iche' Maya kingdom centered at Q'umarkaj reaches its peak before fragmenting under internal rivalries.
Spanish conquistador Pedro de Alvarado invades and defeats the K'iche' and other highland Maya kingdoms, beginning colonial rule.
The Spanish establish the colonial capital of Santiago de los Caballeros, later relocated after disasters.
The Captaincy General of Guatemala is established, governing much of Central America under the Spanish Crown.
Antigua Guatemala becomes the capital and develops into a flourishing center of colonial art, religion, and learning.
A devastating earthquake destroys Antigua Guatemala, prompting relocation of the capital.
Nueva Guatemala de la Asuncion, modern Guatemala City, is founded as the new colonial capital.
Guatemala declares independence from Spain on September 15, ending three centuries of colonial rule.
Guatemala is briefly annexed to the First Mexican Empire under Agustin de Iturbide.
Guatemala joins the Federal Republic of Central America after Mexican annexation collapses.
The Federal Republic of Central America begins to disintegrate amid civil war and regional conflict.
Guatemala emerges as a fully independent republic as the Central American federation dissolves.
Rafael Carrera proclaims the Republic of Guatemala and rules as a conservative strongman shaping the early nation.
The Liberal Revolution led by Justo Rufino Barrios overthrows conservative rule and ushers in coffee-export modernization.
President Barrios enacts sweeping liberal reforms, secularizing institutions and promoting large coffee plantations.
The United Fruit Company begins operations in Guatemala, gaining vast influence over land, railways, and politics.
Jorge Ubico becomes president and rules as an authoritarian dictator favoring foreign business interests.
The October Revolution ousts Ubico and ushers in a decade of democratic reform known as the Ten Years of Spring.
Juan Jose Arevalo is elected president and enacts social, labor, and educational reforms while Guatemala joins the United Nations as a founding member.
Jacobo Arbenz becomes president and launches land reform redistributing uncultivated estates to landless peasants.
A CIA-backed coup overthrows President Arbenz, installing a military government and ending the democratic reform era.
The Guatemalan Civil War begins as leftist insurgents take up arms against successive military governments.
A catastrophic earthquake kills more than 23,000 people and devastates Guatemala City and surrounding regions.
General Efrain Rios Montt seizes power and oversees a scorched-earth campaign with mass killings of Maya communities.
A new constitution is adopted and civilian rule returns with the election of President Vinicio Cerezo.
Maya activist Rigoberta Menchu is awarded the Nobel Peace Prize for her work defending indigenous rights.
The Peace Accords are signed, ending a 36-year civil war that killed an estimated 200,000 people.
A UN-backed truth commission report concludes that state forces committed acts of genocide against the Maya population.
The International Commission against Impunity in Guatemala begins work to combat corruption and organized crime.
Former dictator Rios Montt is convicted of genocide, though the verdict is later annulled on procedural grounds.
Mass anti-corruption protests force President Otto Perez Molina to resign amid the La Linea customs fraud scandal.
Volcan de Fuego erupts violently, killing hundreds and burying communities near Antigua under pyroclastic flows.
Guatemala signs a controversial safe-third-country migration agreement with the United States.
Anti-corruption candidate Bernardo Arevalo is inaugurated president after a contested election and attempts to block his victory.
